attributes.stem:
A 21 year-old male presents to his general practitioner with an unequally sized left finger. He also has streaky, pigmented and rough naevi in both legs and feet. He says that these have been present for as long as he can remember. He has no previously diagnosed medical conditions nor is he on any regular medications.

On physical examination, he is noted to have mild scoliosis and bilateral plantar hyperplasia. His cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are normal. He has no joint pain nor any limitations in their range of motion.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This clinical stem represents a mild case of Proteus syndrome; a very rare genetic disorder, where there is an overgrowth of various tissues including skin, bone, muscle and fatty tissues. It typically results in asymmetric overgrowth. Symptoms of Proteus syndrome can be quite diverse and can affect different parts of the body in different ways. Other features can include scoliosis, cerebriform plantar hyperplasia, and macrocephaly. The overgrowth in individuals with Proteus syndrome is, for most parts of the skeleton (except congenital hemimegencephaly), absent or minimal at birth. It does not typically manifest until age six to 18 months, with onset being as late 12 years old. Genetic testing can be used to confirm the diagnosis. It is noted that many individuals with Proteus syndrome have quite debilitating overgrowth leading to significant physical disability.

Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome can lead to a wide range of symptoms and complications. The most common features include hemihyperplasia, macroglossia, macrocephaly, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and abdominal wall defects (such as umbilical hernia or omphalocele). It is commonly diagnosed shortly after birth or in early childhood.

Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disease that results in excessive linear growth of the long bones, leading to tall stature and joint laxity. Other important clinical features include ophthalmological complications such as myopia, disproportionately sized limbs and cardiac anomalies

Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune disease that can result in deformities of the hands such as swan neck deformity, Boutonniere deformity, and ulnar deviation.

Scleroderma is an autoimmune connective tissue disease that can result in calcinosis, Raynaud's phenomenon, oesophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly, and telangiectasia.
learningPoints: For rare genetic conditions, prompt and early referral to a medical geneticist and genetic counsellor is beneficial
Proteus syndrome is a rare genetic disorder, where there is an overgrowth of various tissues including skin, bone, and muscle
references: Neri G, Boccuto L, Stevenson RE. Overgrowth Syndromes: A Clinical Guide. Oxford University Press, New York, NY; 2019:39-63.
Dietz H. Marfan Syndrome. NORD Guide to Rare Disorders. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins. Philadelphia, PA. 2003:218-9.
